title: TenThousandShotChallenge
tui: Shot-logging mobile app (Flutter/Firebase) - log a session in under 10s, see cumulative progress.
tagline: A simple way to track the 10,000 Shot Challenge.
purpose: Players needed a way to log real sessions and see tangible progress instead of guessing. The challenge is about discipline and repetition.
constraints: Cross-platform requirement with real-time sync, multiple auth providers, and structured logging that had to scale to thousands of cumulative entries per user.
decisions: Firebase and Firestore for real-time sync and structured session data. Flutter for a single codebase across iOS and Android without sacrificing native performance.
impact: Players can open the app, log shots in seconds, and see cumulative progress clearly - no guessing, no friction.
story: The 10,000 Shot Challenge is a proven training method, but most players track it in notes apps or lose count. I built a mobile app that makes session logging take under ten seconds and surfaces cumulative progress in a way that actually motivates. Google and Apple auth, Firestore data modeling for per-session tracking, and real-time sync across devices - all wrapped in a UI designed for athletes who want to log and move on.

title: The Pond
tui: WordPress membership platform (LearnDash/MemberPress) - content hierarchy first, zero admin friction.
tagline: A structured membership platform for hockey training.
purpose: Online hockey advice is scattered and unstructured. The Pond created a learning path with gated content and clear progression for committed players.
constraints: Built on WordPress to stay maintainable for non-technical admins. Membership logic, course gating, and payment processing all had to integrate cleanly without custom infrastructure.
decisions: MemberPress for membership logic, LearnDash for course architecture. Designed the content hierarchy first so navigation was intuitive before writing a single line of integration code.
impact: Players always know what to do next. Admins manage the platform without technical gymnastics. Content governance reduced ongoing overhead significantly.
story: The Pond is How To Hockey's structured membership platform. The challenge wasn't just the tech - it was designing a content hierarchy that felt intuitive to players at every level. I architected the course and gating logic in LearnDash and MemberPress, with custom integrations for onboarding and admin workflows optimized to require zero technical intervention for ongoing content management.

title: Group of Seven Trail App
tui: Trail companion app (Flutter/C++) - Bluetooth beacons surface context at exactly the right moment.
tagline: Interactive trail exploration built for real hikers.
purpose: A companion app for the Lake Superior Group of Seven Trail that connected location, history, and movement - making the trail itself interactive without being distracting.
constraints: Had to work in remote conditions with slow connectivity. Bluetooth beacon triggering needed to be performant without draining battery. UI designed for outdoor, active use.
decisions: Flutter for cross-platform with C++ native integrations for Bluetooth performance. Firebase/Firestore for slow but reliable data sync. Proximity-based content triggers over explicit navigation - the trail guides the experience, not menus.
impact: Relevant trail information surfaces automatically at the right moment. Hikers stay in the experience instead of digging through menus.
story: This project combined software engineering with physical exploration. I built a mobile app for the Lake Superior Group of Seven Trail that used Bluetooth beacon scanning to trigger contextual content based on a hiker's exact location. The UX constraint was strict: information should appear when it's relevant, not when the user goes looking for it. C++ native integrations handled the Bluetooth layer for performance, while Flutter handled the UI and map layers.

title: Group of Seven Trail Website
tui: WordPress-based trail website - easy content management meets Lake Superior's vast beauty.
tagline: A digital reflection of Lake Superior's majesty.
purpose: The Group of Seven Trail needed a modern website that could be easily managed by non-technical staff while reflecting the vast beauty of Lake Superior and the legacy of the Group of Seven artists.
constraints: Had to replace an outdated template-based site. Content management needed to be accessible to staff without technical backgrounds. Design had to complement the natural landscape without overwhelming visitors looking for practical trail information.
decisions: WordPress for ease of content management and maintainability. Custom theme design centered on high-quality imagery of Lake Superior and the trail. Clear information architecture balancing visual storytelling with practical visitor information - trail details, supportive businesses, volunteer opportunities, and donation pathways.
impact: Staff can update trail information, news, and events without technical intervention. Visitors experience the beauty of Lake Superior digitally before they arrive. The site serves as both inspiration and practical resource.
story: The Group of Seven Trail Association needed to move beyond their outdated template-based website. The new site needed to be two things at once: a practical resource for hikers and visitors, and a visual celebration of Lake Superior's rugged beauty that inspired the Group of Seven artists. I built a custom WordPress theme that prioritized large, high-quality imagery of the trail and lake, while maintaining clear navigation for essential information like trail maps, supportive businesses, and volunteer opportunities. The content management system was designed so staff could update trail conditions, post news, and manage events without any technical knowledge - critical for a non-profit with limited resources.

title: NextShift
tui: Real-time feature voting platform (Firebase) - idea to ranked result in under 20 seconds.
purpose: Feature prioritization shouldn't be based on guesswork. Users should have a direct voice in what gets built next - and be able to see how their input stacks up.
constraints: Real-time voting had to be consistent across concurrent users. Categorization needed to stay clean without heavy admin overhead. Submit-to-visible flow needed to be under 20 seconds.
decisions: Firebase for real-time voting state. Clean separation between user-facing submission flow and admin moderation tooling. Efficient rendering for dynamic ranked lists that update live.
impact: Users can submit an idea in under 20 seconds and immediately see where it stands. Roadmap decisions became data-driven instead of gut-driven.
story: NextShift is a feedback and feature voting platform. The core problem: anyone can collect feedback, but most teams don't structure it in a way that's useful for prioritization. I built the data model around structured categorization and real-time voting, with a submission flow designed to take under 20 seconds from idea to visible. Firebase handles real-time consistency; the UI handles making complex ranking feel simple.

title: Skill Drills
tui: Customizable training tracker (Flutter/C++) - user-defined routines and metrics, no rigid structure.
purpose: Training isn't one-size-fits-all. I wanted to build something that lets users define their own metrics instead of forcing them into a predetermined structure.
constraints: Flexible data models that could handle varied skill categories without becoming chaotic. Performance-optimized rendering for users with large datasets. The freedom had to feel structured, not open-ended.
decisions: User-defined routines and metric definitions backed by dynamic data models. C++ for the computation layer to keep rendering fast even with large session histories.
impact: Users get the structure of a tracking app with the flexibility of a spreadsheet - without the spreadsheet overhead.
story: Skill Drills came from a simple frustration: most tracking apps either do too much or lock you into their definition of progress. I built a customizable app where users define their own routines and metrics. The data model supports arbitrary skill categories while keeping the UI clean. C++ handles the performance-sensitive computation layer so rendering stays fast even with hundreds of logged sessions.

title: timmies-helper
tui: Fork + Value Over Replacement column for Tim's Hockey Challenge - best pick in each tier, at a glance.
tagline: A fork extended with Value Over Replacement to find the best pick in each tier.
purpose: The original timmies-helper tool helped with Tim Hortons Hockey Challenge picks. I forked it to add a missing piece: a Value Over Replacement (VOR) column that makes the best value pick in each tier immediately obvious.
constraints: Built on top of another developer's existing codebase, so the addition had to integrate cleanly without disrupting the existing logic or UI conventions.
decisions: Added a VOR column that computes each player's value relative to the baseline replacement-level pick in their tier - the same concept used in fantasy sports drafting. Surfaced as a dedicated column so users can sort and compare at a glance.
impact: VOR turns a list of picks into a ranked value decision. Users can immediately see which pick has the best return relative to who else is available in that tier.
story: timmies-helper was built by another developer to assist with Tim Hortons Hockey Challenge picks. I forked it to add Value Over Replacement - a metric borrowed from fantasy sports drafting that scores each player relative to the baseline replacement-level pick in their tier. The fork adds a single column, but it changes the decision from 'who's good' to 'who's the best value right now.' Built to integrate cleanly into the existing codebase without refactoring what was already working.

title: VideoScraper
tui: URL-in, file-out video download utility (React/Node.js) - strict frontend/backend separation, zero friction.
purpose: Sometimes you need a tool that does one thing well. Paste a link, click once, get the file.
constraints: CORS handling required a proxy backend. The frontend had to stay completely decoupled from the backend routing logic. No unnecessary surface area.
decisions: Node.js and Express for the proxy backend. React and Vite for the frontend. Strict separation of responsibilities - the frontend knows nothing about the download mechanics.
impact: One URL in. One file out. Zero friction between intention and result.
story: VideoScraper is a focused utility - a React/Vite frontend backed by a Node.js/Express proxy that handles CORS and request routing. The design principle was strict single-responsibility: the frontend does not know how the backend works, and the backend does not care about the UI. Users paste a video URL and get a file. The system does the rest.
